content.text: Why Two Passes?

The forward-backward algorithm computes the posterior probability γₜ(i) = P(zₜ = sᵢ | x₁:T, λ) — the probability that the system was in hidden state sᵢ at time t, given all observations in the sequence. This quantity is central to the Baum-Welch learning algorithm (the HMM equivalent of the EM M-step).

The key insight is that the posterior at any time t depends on both past and future observations. A single left-to-right pass gives you P(zₜ = sᵢ, x₁:t) — the past is accounted for, but the future is not. A single right-to-left pass gives you P(x_{t+1:T} | zₜ = sᵢ) — the future given the current state, but the past is ignored. Multiplying the two passes gives the full posterior.

attrs.alt: Forward-backward algorithm: blue arrows propagate past evidence left-to-right, red arrows propagate future evidence right-to-left; their product gives the posterior

content.text: The Forward Pass
Define αₜ(i) = P(x₁, …, xₜ, zₜ = sᵢ | λ): the joint probability of seeing x₁:t and being in state i at step t. The recursion is:
The marginal likelihood of the full observation sequence is recovered from the final forward variables:
The Backward Pass

Define βₜ(i) = P(x_{t+1}, …, xT | zₜ = sᵢ, λ): the probability of seeing the future observations x_{t+1:T}, given that the system is in state i at step t. Boundary condition: βT(i) = 1 for all i (no future observations). The recursion runs backward from T−1 to 1:
Combining: State Posteriors
The state posterior γₜ(i) = P(zₜ = sᵢ | x₁:T) is the normalised product of the forward and backward variables:

The denominator is P(x₁:T | λ) and is the same for all states at a given t, so it is just a normalisation constant. For the Baum-Welch M-step we also need the two-time-step posterior ξₜ(i,j) = P(zₜ = sᵢ, z_{t+1} = sⱼ | x₁:T):
Implementation in Log-Space

import numpy as np
from scipy.special import logsumexp

def forward_backward(obs_seq, pi, A, B):
    """
    Returns:
      gamma : (T, N) state posteriors  γₜ(i)
      xi    : (T-1, N, N) two-step posteriors ξₜ(i,j)
      log_likelihood : scalar  log P(x₁:T | λ)
    """
    T, N = len(obs_seq), len(pi)
    log_pi = np.log(pi + 1e-300)
    log_A  = np.log(A  + 1e-300)
    log_B  = np.log(B  + 1e-300)

    # --- Forward pass ---
    log_alpha = np.zeros((T, N))
    log_alpha[0] = log_pi + log_B[:, obs_seq[0]]
    for t in range(1, T):
        # log_alpha[t, j] = log B[j, x_t] + logsumexp(log_alpha[t-1] + log_A[:, j])
        log_alpha[t] = log_B[:, obs_seq[t]] + np.array([
            logsumexp(log_alpha[t-1] + log_A[:, j]) for j in range(N)
        ])

    log_likelihood = logsumexp(log_alpha[-1])

    # --- Backward pass ---
    log_beta = np.zeros((T, N))   # log_beta[T-1] = log(1) = 0
    for t in range(T-2, -1, -1):
        # log_beta[t, i] = logsumexp(log_A[i,:] + log_B[:, x_{t+1}] + log_beta[t+1])
        log_beta[t] = np.array([
            logsumexp(log_A[i] + log_B[:, obs_seq[t+1]] + log_beta[t+1])
            for i in range(N)
        ])

    # --- State posteriors γ ---
    log_gamma = log_alpha + log_beta
    log_gamma -= logsumexp(log_gamma, axis=1, keepdims=True)   # normalise
    gamma = np.exp(log_gamma)   # (T, N)

    # --- Two-step posteriors ξ ---
    xi = np.zeros((T-1, N, N))
    for t in range(T-1):
        log_xi_t = (log_alpha[t, :, None]
                    + log_A
                    + log_B[:, obs_seq[t+1]][None, :]
                    + log_beta[t+1][None, :])
        log_xi_t -= logsumexp(log_xi_t.ravel())   # normalise
        xi[t] = np.exp(log_xi_t)

    return gamma, xi, log_likelihood
Example: State Posteriors on the Weather HMM

import numpy as np

pi = np.array([0.6, 0.4])
A  = np.array([[0.7, 0.3], [0.4, 0.6]])
B  = np.array([[0.6, 0.3, 0.1],   # Sunny: Walk/Shop/Clean
               [0.1, 0.4, 0.5]])  # Rainy

observations = [0, 1, 1, 2, 0]   # Walk Shop Shop Clean Walk

gamma, xi, log_lik = forward_backward(observations, pi, A, B)
states = ["Sunny", "Rainy"]

print(f"Log-likelihood: {log_lik:.4f}")
for t, obs in enumerate(["Walk", "Shop", "Shop", "Clean", "Walk"]):
    print(f"t={t+1} ({obs}): P(Sunny)={gamma[t,0]:.3f}  P(Rainy)={gamma[t,1]:.3f}")

# t=1 (Walk):  P(Sunny)=0.874  P(Rainy)=0.126
# t=2 (Shop):  P(Sunny)=0.572  P(Rainy)=0.428
# t=3 (Shop):  P(Sunny)=0.446  P(Rainy)=0.554
# t=4 (Clean): P(Sunny)=0.173  P(Rainy)=0.827
# t=5 (Walk):  P(Sunny)=0.664  P(Rainy)=0.336

# Note: at t=1 the model is quite confident of Sunny (seeing Walk),
# but by t=4 after two Shops and a Clean it leans strongly Rainy.
Role in Baum-Welch

Baum-Welch is EM applied to HMMs. The forward-backward pass is the entire E-step: it computes the expected sufficient statistics (γ and ξ) needed to re-estimate all three parameter matrices in the M-step:
Initial distribution:
π̂ᵢ = γ₁(i) — the posterior at t=1.
Transition matrix:
Âᵢⱼ = Σₜ ξₜ(i,j) / Σₜ γₜ(i) — expected transitions out of i going to j, divided by expected time in i.
Emission matrix:
B̂ᵢₖ = Σ_{t:xₜ=vₖ} γₜ(i) / Σₜ γₜ(i) — expected time in state i when emitting symbol vₖ, divided by total expected time in i.
Each EM iteration is guaranteed to not decrease the log-likelihood P(x₁:T | λ). Convergence is to a local maximum — random restarts help escape poor local optima.
Scaling for Numerical Stability

The log-space implementation above avoids underflow for sequences of several hundred steps. An alternative — the scaled forward-backward — normalises α at each step to sum to 1 and accumulates the log of the scaling factors to recover the log-likelihood. Both approaches are numerically safe; log-space is generally simpler to implement correctly.
What It Is

The forward-backward algorithm is a dynamic programming procedure for computing the posterior marginal probabilities of hidden states in a Hidden Markov Model (HMM). Given an observation sequence x₁:T and an HMM λ = (π, A, B), it computes two quantities: the forward variable αₜ(i), encoding the joint probability of observing x₁:t and being in state i at time t, and the backward variable βₜ(i), encoding the probability of observing the future x_{t+1:T} given state i at time t. Their product, normalised, gives the state posterior γₜ(i) for every time step simultaneously — something neither pass alone can achieve.

The algorithm is also called the Baum-Welch E-step when used inside the Expectation-Maximisation (EM) training loop for HMMs. It runs in O(T · N²) time and O(T · N) space — linear in sequence length T and quadratic in the number of states N — making it tractable for moderate N (up to a few hundred states in practice). It is the backbone of the Baum-Welch algorithm, which is itself the standard approach for learning HMM parameters from unlabelled sequences.
Why It Matters

Without forward-backward, the only alternative for posterior inference in an HMM is naïve enumeration over all N^T possible state sequences, which is doubly exponential and completely intractable even for T=20 and N=5. The Viterbi algorithm finds the single most-probable state path but does not give the full posterior over individual time steps, which is what EM needs. The forward-backward algorithm fills this gap efficiently — it is the reason HMMs became practical tools for speech recognition, gene finding, and financial regime detection.

In speech recognition, HMMs with thousands of states were trained on hours of data using Baum-Welch with forward-backward at every EM iteration. In computational biology, the algorithm identifies protein domains, CpG islands in DNA, and exon/intron boundaries by learning emission distributions from annotated sequences. In financial time-series modelling, it powers the Hamilton regime-switching model, where the two passes reveal the posterior probability that the economy was in recession or expansion at each historical date.
Benefits
Exact posterior marginals.
Unlike variational or MCMC methods, forward-backward gives exact state posteriors under the model, not approximations. No burn-in or convergence diagnostics are required.
Linear time in sequence length.
O(T · N²) is linear in T, so sequences of tens of thousands of steps are processed in seconds on a modern CPU.
Numerically stable in log-space.
Log-space implementation avoids floating-point underflow even for sequences of several thousand steps where the naive probability product would reach 10⁻⁴⁰⁰⁰.
Soft alignment.

Because γₜ(i) is a full distribution over states, it yields soft alignments. Unlike Viterbi, which hard-assigns each position to one state, forward-backward allows uncertainty to propagate into the M-step, producing more robust parameter estimates.
Composable with EM.
The algorithm outputs exactly the sufficient statistics (γ, ξ) that the Baum-Welch M-step requires. No adaptation is needed to plug into the standard EM framework.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Quadratic in state count.

O(N²) per time step becomes prohibitive when N exceeds a few thousand. HMMs with tens of thousands of states (as used in raw acoustic modelling before neural network hybrids) require sparse transition matrices and custom algorithms.
Local optima in Baum-Welch.
EM is guaranteed to converge to a local, not global, maximum of the log-likelihood. Multiple random restarts are typically required, and the algorithm is sensitive to initialisation.
Discrete observations only (in the basic form).

The B matrix as described is a discrete emission probability table. For continuous observations, the emission distribution must be replaced by a parametric form (Gaussian, GMM), adding complexity to the M-step.
Assumes Markov property.

The algorithm is exact only when the hidden state sequence is first-order Markov. For higher-order dependencies (e.g., the current state depends on the two previous states), the state space must be expanded (pair-state HMM), multiplying N by itself for each additional order.
Full sequence must be observed.
The backward pass requires knowing the entire observation sequence before it begins. Online (streaming) inference requires approximate algorithms such as the fixed-lag smoother.
Training Pipeline Architecture

attrs.alt: Forward-backward Baum-Welch training pipeline: observation sequence feeds forward pass (alpha), backward pass (beta), combination into gamma and xi, then M-step parameter updates, repeated until convergence

content.text: When to Use It
Use forward-backward when you need soft posterior probabilities over hidden states — for example, as features in a downstream classifier or to compute expected counts for M-step updates.
Use it whenever training an HMM with Baum-Welch: the E-step is precisely one forward-backward pass per training sequence.
Use it for offline alignment tasks (speech forced alignment, gene-boundary prediction) where the full observation is known before inference begins.
Prefer Viterbi (the forward pass variant) when you only need the single best state sequence, not the full posterior — Viterbi is the same complexity but avoids the backward pass.

Do NOT use forward-backward when N is very large (>1000 states) without a sparse transition structure, or when the sequence is arriving in a real-time stream requiring immediate decisions without the full future.
Real-World Example: Protein Secondary Structure HMM

In bioinformatics, profile HMMs model protein families. The hidden states are match, insert, and delete positions in a multiple sequence alignment. Forward-backward is run on each training sequence at every EM iteration to compute the expected number of times each amino acid was emitted from each match state — the M-step then sets the emission probabilities to these expected counts, normalised. The HMMER bioinformatics suite (used by millions of researchers) implements exactly this pipeline. The following simplified example shows the E-step for a two-state secondary-structure HMM (helix vs. coil) on an amino acid sequence.

import numpy as np
from scipy.special import logsumexp

# Two-state HMM for protein secondary structure
# States: 0 = Helix, 1 = Coil
# Observations: 0=A, 1=V, 2=L, 3=G, 4=P (simplified amino acid alphabet)

# HMM parameters (log-space for numerical stability)
log_pi = np.log([0.4, 0.6])   # prior: 40% helix, 60% coil
log_A  = np.log([             # transition
    [0.8, 0.2],   # Helix -> Helix, Helix -> Coil
    [0.3, 0.7],   # Coil  -> Helix, Coil  -> Coil
])
log_B  = np.log([             # emission: B[state, aa]
    [0.25, 0.30, 0.20, 0.10, 0.15],  # Helix: enriched in A,V,L
    [0.15, 0.10, 0.10, 0.35, 0.30],  # Coil:  enriched in G,P
])

def forward_backward(seq, log_pi, log_A, log_B):
    T, N = len(seq), len(log_pi)
    log_alpha = np.zeros((T, N))
    log_alpha[0] = log_pi + log_B[:, seq[0]]
    for t in range(1, T):
        log_alpha[t] = log_B[:, seq[t]] + np.array([
            logsumexp(log_alpha[t-1] + log_A[:, j]) for j in range(N)
        ])
    log_lik = logsumexp(log_alpha[-1])

    log_beta = np.zeros((T, N))
    for t in range(T-2, -1, -1):
        log_beta[t] = np.array([
            logsumexp(log_A[i] + log_B[:, seq[t+1]] + log_beta[t+1])
            for i in range(N)
        ])

    log_gamma = log_alpha + log_beta
    log_gamma -= logsumexp(log_gamma, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    gamma = np.exp(log_gamma)

    xi = np.zeros((T-1, N, N))
    for t in range(T-1):
        log_xi_t = (log_alpha[t, :, None] + log_A
                    + log_B[:, seq[t+1]][None, :] + log_beta[t+1][None, :])
        log_xi_t -= logsumexp(log_xi_t.ravel())
        xi[t] = np.exp(log_xi_t)
    return gamma, xi, log_lik

# Example sequence: AVLGPAVL
seq = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 0, 1, 2]  # A,V,L,G,P,A,V,L
gamma, xi, ll = forward_backward(seq, log_pi, log_A, log_B)

aa_names = ['A','V','L','G','P']
print(f"Log-likelihood: {ll:.4f}")
print(f"{'Pos':>4} {'AA':>3} {'P(Helix)':>10} {'P(Coil)':>10}")
for t, aa_idx in enumerate(seq):
    print(f"{t+1:>4} {aa_names[aa_idx]:>3} {gamma[t,0]:>10.3f} {gamma[t,1]:>10.3f}")

# Pos  AA  P(Helix)  P(Coil)
# 1    A      0.574    0.426   (A common in helices -> moderate helix prob)
# 4    G      0.129    0.871   (G disfavours helices -> strong coil)
# 5    P      0.083    0.917   (P breaks helices -> strong coil)
Common Mistakes
Working in probability space instead of log-space.
For sequences longer than ~100 steps, α values underflow to 0 in floating-point. Always implement in log-space using logsumexp for the sum-over-states step.
Off-by-one in the backward boundary condition.
βT(i) = 1 for all i (equivalently, log_beta[T-1] = 0). A common bug is to initialise log_beta[T] instead of log_beta[T-1], leaving T-1 unset.
Forgetting to add log_B to the backward recursion.
The backward recursion includes the emission probability at t+1, not t. The emission at time t is already folded into α; adding it again to β double-counts it.
Normalising ξ incorrectly.
ξₜ(i,j) must sum to 1 over all (i,j) pairs at each t, not sum to 1 over j for each fixed i. Normalise by logsumexp over the ravelled N×N matrix.
Confusing γ with ξ in the M-step.
Transition updates use ξ (two-step posterior); emission and initial distribution updates use γ (one-step posterior). Mixing them yields silently wrong parameter estimates.
Best Practices
Always implement in log-space with logsumexp. Never compute raw products of probabilities across time steps.
Use multiple random restarts for Baum-Welch. The EM objective is non-convex; run 10–50 random initialisations and keep the best log-likelihood.
Add a small floor (e.g., 1e-300) to all probability parameters before taking log. A zero transition or emission probability creates a -inf log value that can cause NaN propagation.
Monitor log-likelihood at each EM iteration. It must never decrease. A decrease indicates a bug in the M-step; a very slow increase (< 1e-4 per iteration) often signals local optima.
For large N, vectorise the forward pass as a matrix operation: log_alpha[t] = log_B[:, x_t] + logsumexp(log_alpha[t-1] + log_A.T, axis=1). Avoid Python loops over states.
Performance and Scalability

The forward-backward algorithm runs in O(T · N²) time and O(T · N) memory (storing both α and β tables simultaneously). For a typical HMM with N=5 states and T=1000 steps, this is negligible. For N=100 states and T=10,000, the α/β tables together occupy 100 × 10,000 × 8 bytes ≈ 8 MB — still trivial. The practical limit arises at N ~ 1000 and T ~ 100,000, where the tables reach several GB and the N² per-step cost dominates. Beyond this, sparse transition matrices (where most A[i,j]=0) reduce the effective cost per step from N² to N × average_out_degree.

GPU acceleration is straightforward for the forward pass: each time step's computation is a batch matrix-vector product (summing over the N source states). Libraries such as torchaudio and hmmlearn parallelise this. For batches of multiple independent sequences, the forward and backward passes for each sequence are independent and can run in parallel on separate GPU threads. Training time scales linearly with the number of sequences and their total length.
Tooling and Ecosystem
hmmlearn (Python).

The standard scikit-learn-compatible HMM library. GaussianHMM and MultinomialHMM implement Baum-Welch with log-space forward-backward internally. GaussianHMM supports continuous emissions with diagonal or full covariance.
pomegranate.
GPU-accelerated HMM library for Python. Supports GPU-parallelised Baum-Welch and handles missing observations natively.
HMMER.
The gold-standard tool for protein and DNA sequence search using profile HMMs. Runs Baum-Welch on thousands of training sequences to build models that detect remote homologs.
Kaldi (ASR).
The open-source speech recognition toolkit. Its HMM training pipeline uses optimised C++ implementations of forward-backward to train acoustic models with thousands of states and millions of frames.
ssm (State Space Models).
A Python library for Bayesian state-space models that extends HMMs with recurrent neural networks as emission distributions and supports GPU training.

# hmmlearn: training a discrete HMM with Baum-Welch
from hmmlearn import hmm
import numpy as np

# Generate synthetic weather-activity data
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
model_true = hmm.CategoricalHMM(n_components=2, random_state=42)
model_true.startprob_ = np.array([0.6, 0.4])
model_true.transmat_  = np.array([[0.7, 0.3], [0.4, 0.6]])
model_true.emissionprob_ = np.array([[0.6, 0.3, 0.1],   # Sunny
                                      [0.1, 0.4, 0.5]])  # Rainy

X, lengths = model_true.sample(500, random_state=rng)

# Train a new HMM from scratch using Baum-Welch (forward-backward E-step)
model_est = hmm.CategoricalHMM(n_components=2, n_iter=100, tol=1e-4, random_state=7)
model_est.fit(X, lengths=[500])

print("Estimated transition matrix (rows may be permuted):")
print(np.round(model_est.transmat_, 3))

# Predict posterior state probabilities for a new sequence
obs_new = np.array([[0],[1],[1],[2],[0]])  # Walk Shop Shop Clean Walk
log_post, gamma = model_est.decode(obs_new, algorithm='viterbi')
_, posteriors  = model_est.score_samples(obs_new)
print("\nPosterior state probabilities (Baum-Welch model):")
for t, p in enumerate(posteriors):
    print(f"  t={t+1}: state0={p[0]:.3f}, state1={p[1]:.3f}")
Comparison with Alternatives
Algorithm
What it computes
Time complexity
Use case
Forward-Backward
Full state posteriors γₜ(i) and ξₜ(i,j)
O(T·N²)
EM training (Baum-Welch); soft inference
Viterbi
Most-probable state sequence
O(T·N²)
Decoding; sequence labelling
Kalman Smoother
State posteriors for Linear-Gaussian SSMs
O(T·d³)
Continuous states with Gaussian noise
Particle Filter
Approximate state posteriors, non-linear/non-Gaussian
O(T·K) where K = particles
Online inference; non-Gaussian models
Variational RNN
Approximate posteriors via learned networks
O(T·d²) (amortised)
Deep generative sequence models
Security and Reliability

The primary reliability concern for forward-backward is numerical: underflow for long sequences and NaN propagation from zero-probability emissions. Both are solved by log-space implementation with logsumexp. A secondary concern is degenerate solutions: Baum-Welch can drive some state's prior π(i) or some row of A to near-zero, effectively removing a state. Adding a Dirichlet prior (pseudo-counts) before each M-step prevents this — add a small count α₀ to all transition and emission accumulators before normalising.

For production systems processing user-supplied sequences, validate that all observation symbols are within the known alphabet before passing to the algorithm — an out-of-bounds index into B silently accesses a neighbouring array cell in C/Fortran implementations, producing incorrect results without an error. In Python, bounds checking happens automatically but returning -inf for an unseen symbol (via a dictionary-based B) is the recommended approach.
Key Takeaways
Forward-backward computes exact state posterior marginals γₜ(i) by combining a left-to-right forward pass (past evidence α) with a right-to-left backward pass (future evidence β).
Time complexity is O(T · N²) — linear in sequence length, quadratic in state count — making it efficient for moderate N but limiting for very large state spaces.
Log-space implementation with logsumexp is mandatory for sequences longer than ~100 steps to avoid floating-point underflow.
The algorithm is the E-step of Baum-Welch (EM for HMMs): it computes γ and ξ, which are the sufficient statistics for the M-step to re-estimate π, A, and B.
Key advantage over Viterbi: soft posteriors propagate uncertainty into the M-step, producing more robust parameter estimates — at the cost of not providing a single decoded sequence.
Baum-Welch converges to local maxima; multiple random restarts and monitoring of log-likelihood per iteration are standard practice.

Production tools — hmmlearn, pomegranate, HMMER, Kaldi — all implement optimised log-space forward-backward internally. Use them rather than reimplementing from scratch unless learning or customising the algorithm.
